articles.title: Here’s Why Your Oven Door Has Tiny Dots on It
articles.content:
你有没有过这样的感觉：等着曲奇饼干变成棕色，或者等着千层面冒泡，一边（不那么）耐心地透过烤箱门往里瞅。然后你的目光落在了一个你可能见过数百次却从未真正留意的东西上：玻璃边缘的一圈小黑点。

这些东西太常见了，以至于我们大多数人都不会去质疑它们。但一旦你注意到了，就很难不好奇：它们是装饰性的吗？是某种温度传感器吗？还是制造商觉得玻璃看起来太单调了？

事实上，这些小点并不是装饰。它们是一个精心设计的功能——而且有着出乎意料的重要作用。为了了解真正的情况，我请教了利哈伊大学材料科学与工程教授、玻璃科学与技术领域顶尖专家希曼苏·贾恩博士，以及舒适家电维修公司的所有者兼经营者丹尼斯·戈迪努克。

继续阅读，深入了解这个你再也不会用同样眼光看待的聪明厨房功能。

这些小黑点被称为釉点，或陶瓷釉料图案。它们在制造过程中被印刷在烤箱门外部玻璃的内表面，所以你从外面能看到但摸不到。

“釉点是一个非常通用的术语，指的是玻璃粉末，”贾恩解释道。所以这个过程始于研磨成细粉的玻璃，混合成可印刷的糊状物。根据家电制造商博世的发言人所说，现代烤箱门的釉点由含有玻璃助熔剂、黑色颜料和有机粘合剂的陶瓷油墨制成。这种混合物在玻璃回火之前通过丝网印刷印上去，将图案永久熔合在表面。

釉点通常在玻璃边缘形成一圈实心黑色边框，然后逐渐过渡到更小、更稀疏的点，向中心方向延伸。

黑点的用途远不止装饰门面。事实上，它们有许多实际作用，如下所述。

玻璃是制作烤箱窗口的理想材料，因为它让你能看到内部，同时能承受高温。但是贾恩指出，“它很容易破碎。”问题不仅仅是烤箱会发热，而是玻璃的不同部位可能达到非常不同的温度。面向烤箱的一面可能变得极热，而外面的一面仍然凉得多。“任何材料在承受大的温差时，都会承受高应力，因为受热部分会膨胀，而冷却部分仍然是固定的，”贾恩解释道。

釉料图案有助于平衡这些温度差异，特别是在玻璃与金属门框连接处。“这些黑点的目的基本上是减少这种温度变化，”贾恩说。“所以不是温度突然变化，而是温度逐渐变化。”

戈迪努克说，从本质上讲，釉点“通过让门材料更均匀地加热和冷却来帮助提高玻璃耐用性。因此，它能防止热冲击。”这难道不是一个有趣的事实吗？！

通过减少热应力，釉点有助于使烤箱门更耐用——但这只是好处的一部分。虽然防止玻璃破裂很重要，但你也不希望玻璃变得太烫而无法触碰。博世的发言人表示，陶瓷釉点有助于更均匀地分布辐射热在外层玻璃面板上，减少局部热点，并帮助将外部门保持在消费者安全温度限制内。

戈迪努克补充说，改进的热管理也让家电本身受益。“釉点有助于维持烤箱内部温度，并在此过程中降低能耗，”他说。

釉料图案还有一个审美目的——遮盖将烤箱门固定在一起的粘合剂、密封圈、螺丝和支架。根据博世的说法，陶瓷边框隐藏了结构性玻璃边缘和其他内部硬件，同时减少“彩虹效应”——当光通过多层经过处理的玻璃时可能出现的光学现象——创造更干净、更统一的外观。

渐变的点阵图案在外观和功能之间取得了平衡。“其背后的科学原理是，黑色以吸收辐射、热和光而著称，”贾恩说。“它吸收热量，然后重新辐射”回烤箱内部。“但你不想把整个窗户都做成黑色，因为那样就看不到里面了，”他补充道。“你就会失去整个目的。”

不会！虽然釉点帮助烤箱门管理热量，但它们不会改变食物的烹饪方式或热量在烤箱内部的循环方式。相反，它们通过保护玻璃、保持热量和维持清晰的视野，让门发挥预期作用。

不会。早期的烤箱通常只有简单的透明玻璃面板，没有如今常见的陶瓷釉料图案。

不过有趣的是，陶瓷釉料技术比现代烤箱古老得多。制造商在20世纪中叶首次在汽车挡风玻璃上使用陶瓷釉料涂层，以提高耐用性、保护粘合剂，并使窗框边缘看起来更美观。（如果你一直在想的话，没错，那就是你见过那些点的另一个地方！）

当烤箱制造商在20世纪70年代推出更大的观察窗和更节能的设计时，他们将这项技术适应到了家用电器上。1975年，Shatterproof Glass Corporation获得了烤箱门专利，该烤箱门在外层玻璃面板上采用陶瓷釉料涂层，以帮助将热量从门辐射出去，同时保持可见性。

如今，陶瓷釉料图案是许多电烤箱和燃气烤箱的标准配置，在那里它们继续帮助管理热量，同时遮盖门内的硬件。

是的。点阵图案是精心设计的，而不是随机放置的。边框出现在最需要热管理的地方——在玻璃与金属框架相接的边缘周围。从那里开始，点逐渐变得更小、更稀疏。

“黑色以吸收辐射、热和光而著称，”贾恩解释道。但如果把整个窗户都做成黑色，就会失去可见性，所以制造商采用了渐变过渡。

据博世发言人称，这种渐变充当热桥。它不是在玻璃涂层部分和透明部分之间创建突然的边界，而是通过逐渐变小的点帮助更均匀地分配热量，同时创造更柔和的视觉过渡。如果没有这种渐变，涂层和未涂层玻璃之间加热的明显差异可能会造成额外的应力。

可能不会。虽然釉点在许多现代烤箱上很常见，但一些制造商使用不同的热管理策略。据戈迪努克说，没有可见釉点的烤箱可能依赖“多层玻璃、低辐射涂层和其他专业隔热材料”。

此外，如果你拥有的是老式烤箱，它可能是在陶瓷釉料图案普及之前制造的。

博世发言人指出，房主通常不需要担心日常清洁会磨损釉点图案，因为它在制造过程中永久熔合在玻璃上。在大多数现代烤箱上，玻璃的印刷面也位于门组件的内部，所以你清洁的是光滑的外表面，而不是陶瓷涂层本身。

戈迪努克建议使用海绵的泡沫面或超细纤维布清洁烤箱门，避免使用研磨性清洁剂、钢丝绒和剃须刀片。这些锋利工具不太可能去除陶瓷釉点本身，但会在玻璃上造成微观划痕，随着时间的推移可能会削弱它。

如果黑色涂层开始剥落，不要认为这是日常清洁造成的。戈迪努克说，剥落可能有很多原因，虽然通常没什么好担心的，但大面积剥落有时可能表明烤箱的搪瓷涂层受损。“一般来说，釉点开始剥落或磨损更多的是美观问题。然而，如果剥落严重，可能是烤箱内部搪瓷涂层损坏的迹象。”

在这种情况下，检查门是否有缺口或裂缝，并咨询制造商的建议。因为陶瓷图案永久熔合在玻璃上，重大损坏通常需要更换玻璃面板——或者，如果找不到可用的更换零件，则需要购买新烤箱。

这些小黑点可能是烤箱上最小的功能之一，但它们在保持家电安全高效运行方面发挥着重要作用。所以下次你透过烤箱窗口看一批曲奇饼干时，你就会知道那些小点不仅仅是装饰；它们正在帮助你的烤箱完成任务。
articles.summary:
You know the feeling: You’re waiting for cookies to brown or lasagna to bubble, while (not so) patiently peeking through the oven door. Then your eyes land on something you’ve probably seen hundreds of times but never really noticed: a band of tiny black dots around the edge of the glass.

They’re so common that most of us never question them. But once you do notice them, it’s hard not to wonder: Are they decorative? Are they some kind of temperature sensor? Or did the manufacturer just decide the glass looked too plain?

As it turns out, those little dots aren’t just window dressing. They’re a carefully engineered feature—and they have a surprisingly important job. To see what’s really cooking, I turned to Himanshu Jain, PhD, a professor of materials science and engineering at Lehigh University and a leading expert in glass science and technology, and Dennis Godynuk, the owner-operator of Comfort Appliance Repair.

Read on for a closer look at this clever kitchen feature you’ll never look at the same way again.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

What are those little black dots on your oven door?

Those tiny black dots are called frits, or a ceramic frit pattern. They’re printed onto the inside surface of the oven door’s outer glass during manufacturing, so you can see them from the outside but can’t touch them.

“Frit is the very general term for glass powder,” Jain explains. So the process begins with finely ground glass that’s mixed into a printable paste. According to a spokesperson for appliance manufacturer Bosch, modern oven-door frits are made from ceramic ink containing a glass flux, black pigments and organic binders. The mixture is screen-printed onto the glass before it’s tempered, permanently fusing the pattern into the surface.

The frits typically form a solid black border around the perimeter of the glass before gradually fading into smaller, more widely spaced dots toward the center.

What’s the purpose of these little black dots?

The black dots do much more than frame the window. In fact, they have a number of practical purposes, as you’ll see.

They help the glass handle extreme temperature changes

Glass is an excellent material for an oven window because it allows you to see inside while tolerating high temperatures. But, as Jain notes, “it breaks easily.” The problem isn’t simply that ovens get hot. It’s that different parts of the glass can reach very different temperatures. The side facing the oven may become extremely hot while the outside remains much cooler. “Anytime material is subjected to a large difference in temperature, it is subjected to high stresses because the hot part expands, whereas the cool part is still fixed,” Jain explains.

The frit pattern helps smooth out those temperature differences, especially where the glass meets the metal door frame. “The purpose of these black dots is to basically reduce this temperature variation,” Jain says. “So it’s not a sudden change in temperature, but a gradual change in temperature.”

Essentially, Godynuk says, frits “help with glass durability by allowing the door material to heat and cool more uniformly. As a result, it prevents thermal shock.” How’s that for an interesting fact?!

They improve safety and help the oven operate efficiently

By reducing thermal stress, frits help make the oven door more durable—but that’s only part of the benefit. While preventing broken glass is important, you also want to prevent the glass from getting too hot to the touch. According to the Bosch spokesperson, the ceramic frits help distribute radiant heat more evenly across the outer glass panel, reducing localized hot spots and helping keep the exterior door within consumer-safety temperature limits.

Godynuk adds that the improved heat management benefits the appliance itself too. “Frits help maintain the oven’s internal temperature and reduce energy consumption in the process,” he says.

They hide hardware while preserving your view

The frit pattern also serves an aesthetic purpose by concealing the adhesives, seals, screws and brackets that hold the oven door together. According to Bosch, the ceramic border hides structural glass edges and other internal hardware while also reducing the “rainbow effect”—an optical phenomenon that can occur when light passes through multiple treated layers of glass—creating a cleaner, more uniform appearance.

The graduated dot pattern balances appearance with function. “The science behind it is that the color black is known for absorbing radiation, heat and light,” says Jain. “It absorbs, and then it radiates” the heat back into the oven’s interior. “But you don’t want to make the whole thing black because then you can’t see through it,” he adds. “You’d lose the whole purpose.”

Do frits affect how your food cooks?

Nope! Although frits help the oven door manage heat, they don’t change how food cooks or how heat circulates inside the oven. Instead, they allow the door to perform as intended by protecting the glass, retaining heat and maintaining a clear view into the oven.

Have oven doors always had frits?

No. Earlier ovens often featured simple, clear glass panels without the ceramic frit patterns common today.

Interestingly, though, ceramic frit technology is much older than modern ovens. Manufacturers first used ceramic frit coatings on car windshields in the mid-20th century to improve durability, protect adhesives and give window edges a finished appearance. (Yep, that’s the other place you’ve seen those dots, in case you were wondering!)

As oven manufacturers introduced larger viewing windows and more energy-efficient designs during the 1970s, they adapted the technology for kitchen appliances. In 1975, Shatterproof Glass Corporation received a patent for an oven door that incorporated ceramic frit coatings on the outer glass panel to help radiate heat away from the door while maintaining visibility.

Today, ceramic frit patterns are standard on many electric and gas ovens, where they continue to help manage heat while concealing the hardware inside the door.

Is there any significance to the pattern of the frits?

Yes. The dot pattern is carefully engineered rather than randomly placed. The border appears where heat management is needed most—around the perimeter where the glass meets the metal frame. From there, the dots become progressively smaller and farther apart.

“The color black is known for absorbing radiation, heat and light,” Jain explains. But making the entire window black would eliminate visibility, so manufacturers instead use a gradual transition.

According to the Bosch spokesperson, that gradient acts as a thermal bridge. Rather than creating an abrupt boundary between the coated and clear sections of the glass, the progressively smaller dots help distribute heat more gradually while also creating a softer visual transition. Without that gradual fade, the sharp difference in heating between the coated and uncoated glass could create additional stress.

Wait—my oven door doesn’t have frits! Is that a problem?

Probably not. While frits are common on many modern ovens, some manufacturers use different heat-management strategies. According to Godynuk, ovens without visible frits may instead rely on “multi-pane glass, low-emissivity coatings and other specialized insulation materials.”

Plus, if you own an older oven, it may have been manufactured before ceramic frit patterns became widespread.

How should you clean the frits?

The Bosch spokesperson notes that homeowners generally don’t need to worry about wearing away the frit pattern during routine cleaning because it’s permanently fused into the glass during manufacturing. On most modern ovens, the printed side of the glass is also positioned inside the door assembly, so you’re cleaning the smooth exterior surface rather than the ceramic coating itself.

Godynuk recommends cleaning your oven door using the foam side of a sponge or a microfiber cloth and avoiding abrasive cleaners, steel wool and razor blades. Those sharp tools aren’t likely to remove the ceramic frit itself, but they can create microscopic scratches in the glass that may weaken it over time.

If the black coating begins to peel, don’t assume routine cleaning is to blame. Godynuk says peeling can have a variety of causes, and while it’s usually nothing to worry about, extensive peeling may sometimes indicate damage to the oven’s enamel coating. “In general, the frits starting to peel or wear off is more of a cosmetic issue. However, if the peeling is significant, it could be a sign of damage to the enamel coating inside the oven.”

In that case, inspect the door for chips or cracks and consult the manufacturer’s recommendations. Because the ceramic pattern is permanently fused into the glass, significant damage typically requires replacing the glass panel—or, if replacement parts are no longer available, purchasing a new oven.

The bottom line

Those tiny black dots may be one of the smallest features on your oven, but they play a big role in keeping the appliance running safely and efficiently. So the next time you’re watching a batch of cookies through the oven window, you’ll know those little dots aren’t just decorative; they’re helping your oven do its job.

Sources:

  • Dennis Godynuk, owner-operator of Comfort Appliance Repair; email interview, July 12, 2026
  • Himanshu Jain, PhD, professor of materials science and engineering at Lehigh University; phone interview, July 14, 2026
  • Bosch press office; email interview, July 14, 2026
  • FreePatentsOnline: “Oven Doors”

Why Cars Have Lines on the Rear Windshield

Why Some Airplane Windows Have Little Holes

What It Means If You See a Star on a Barn

The post Here’s Why Your Oven Door Has Tiny Dots on It appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Watch The Reader’s Digest Escalator Interview with Author Jenny Jackson
articles.content:
珍妮·杰克逊在文学界占据独特地位——她不仅是《菠萝街》和《洗发水效应》两部作品的《纽约时报》畅销书作家，还担任著名出版巨头阿尔弗雷德·A·克诺夫出版社的小说副总裁和编辑总监。虽然她白天为当今最热门的小说担任编辑工作，但杰克逊在业余时间创作自己的畅销女性小说，以机智幽默和情感深度讲述关于家庭、友谊和成年生活复杂现实的故事。

杰克逊2023年的突破性处女作《菠萝街》一经推出便大获成功，被“早安美国”读书俱乐部选中，凭借其对布鲁克林高地特权与家庭关系的犀利而温情刻画赢得了读者的喜爱。随后，她带着备受期待的第二部小说《洗发水效应》重返文坛，该书被“与珍娜共读”节目选中，讲述了短篇小说作家卡罗琳·拉什的故事——她搬到马萨诸塞州格林黑德的海滨小镇，爱上了当地人范·惠特克，一个拥有紧密朋友圈的本地男人。

“很快她就卷入了他们的恶作剧、深夜聚会和狂欢派对，直到她发现范的前女友怀上了他的孩子，”杰克逊说，“在愤怒和悲伤的强烈交织下，她做出了极其糟糕的举动。”

尽管身兼两职，杰克逊表示她的写作始终如一：“我真的喜欢在书中注入欢乐。”这种幽默感和希望在她探讨复杂情感与不完美人物的小说中依然闪耀。

在本期《读者文摘》“扶梯访谈”节目中，Leigh Belz Ray在纽约市巴诺书店与杰克逊对话，探讨了《洗发水效应》、她目前的阅读书单、她钟爱但被低估的文学类型以及更多内容。

观看以上独家采访，然后浏览她的作品和阅读推荐！

在Instagram上查看此帖子

加入免费的《读者文摘》读书俱乐部，获取优质读物、月度讨论、作者问答以及书迷社区。

在《读者文摘》，我们分享最爱的书籍已逾百年。我们曾与苏珊·奥尔良、珍妮特·伊万诺维奇和亚历克斯·哈里等畅销书作家合作，其中哈里荣获普利策奖的《根》最初由该杂志资助并首次发表。《小说精选》（原名《精选版》和《缩写本》）数十年来一直出版小说精选集。我们与詹姆斯·帕特森、露丝·韦尔、克里斯汀·汉娜等文坛巨匠合作过。由图书编辑特蕾西·尼瑟科特主持的《读者文摘》读书俱乐部，通过介绍新锐、畅销和获奖作家，为读者带来更多当今最优秀的小说。本文中，Leigh Belz Ray采访了作者珍妮·杰克逊。我们验证所有事实和数据，以可靠来源支撑，并在时间推移中持续核实以确保准确。了解更多关于我们团队、撰稿人和编辑政策的信息。

《观看〈读者文摘扶梯访谈〉——作者珍妮·杰克逊专访》一文首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
Jenny Jackson has carved out a unique place in the literary world—not only as the New York Times bestselling author of Pineapple Street and The Shampoo Effect, but also as the vice president and editorial director of fiction at the prestigious publishing house Alfred A. Knopf. While she spends her days editing some of today’s biggest novels, Jackson writes her own bestselling women’s fiction after hours, bringing wit and emotional depth to stories about family, friendship and the messy realities of adulthood.

Jackson’s 2023 breakout debut, Pineapple Street, became an instant hit and a Good Morning America Book Club pick, winning readers over with its sharp yet heartfelt take on privilege and family dynamics in Brooklyn Heights. She then returned with her highly anticipated sophomore novel, The Shampoo Effect, a Read with Jenna pick that follows Caroline Lash, a short story writer who moves to the coastal town of Greenhead, Massachusetts, where she falls for Van Whittaker, a local man with a close-knit group of lifelong friends. “Soon she is swept up into their antics, their late nights, their partying, until she discovers that Van’s ex-girlfriend is pregnant with his baby,” says Jackson. “In a potent mix of rage and sorrow, she does something really terrible.”

Despite balancing two demanding careers, Jackson says one thing remains constant in her writing: “I really like to think about infusing my books with joy.” That sense of humor and hope shines through even as her novels tackle complicated emotions and imperfect characters.

For this episode of The Reader’s Digest’s Escalator Interview, Leigh Belz Ray caught up with Jackson at Barnes & Noble in New York City to discuss The Shampoo Effect, the books currently on her reading list, her preferred underrated genre and much more.

Watch the exclusive interview below, and then scroll down to shop her books and reading recommendations!

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Reader’s Digest (@readersdigest)

Join the free Reader’s Digest Book Club for great reads, monthly discussions, author Q&As and a community of book lovers.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’ve been sharing our favorite books for over 100 years. We’ve worked with bestselling authors including Susan Orlean, Janet Evanovich and Alex Haley, whose Pulitzer Prize–winning Roots grew out of a project funded by and originally published in the magazine. Through Fiction Favorites (formerly Select Editions and Condensed Books), Reader’s Digest has been publishing anthologies of abridged novels for decades. We’ve worked with some of the biggest names in fiction, including James Patterson, Ruth Ware, Kristin Hannah and more. The Reader’s Digest Book Club, helmed by Books Editor Tracey Neithercott, introduces readers to even more of today’s best fiction by upcoming, bestselling and award-winning authors. For this piece, Leigh Belz Ray interviewed author Jenny Jackson. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: EXCLUSIVE! Author Marissa Stapley Talks About the Brand-New Lucky TV Series—And Why You Can’t Help but Root for Her Heroine
articles.content:
玛丽莎·斯特普利创作《幸运》这本书的灵感来源并非什么幸运的契机。2019年，这位作家正在构思一部小说，但感到自己缺乏动力和专注力，无法坚持下去。“那些角色太复杂、太悲伤了——我知道这样写不行，”她说道。当时她的母亲瓦莱丽正处于癌症晚期，却给了她所需的鼓励。“她一直希望我在事业上勇往直前，即使我在照顾她的时候也是如此，”她回忆道。“所以她说，‘那你想点别的事情吧。你总是有主意的。’”

斯特普利很快构思出一个引人入胜的惊悚故事，讲述一位身为世界级罪犯的女主人公。这个故事后来成为畅销书，并在2021年12月被选为里斯读书俱乐部的推荐书目。如今，《幸运》已被改编成Apple TV+的七集电视剧，于7月15日首播。

安雅·泰勒-乔伊（《后翼弃兵》）饰演幸运·安德森，一个改过自新的骗子，从她那不务正业的父亲（蒂莫西·奥利芬特饰）那里学会了诈骗的艺术。在一次失败的抢劫后，她开始逃亡，既要躲避联邦调查局，也要躲避一个黑帮老大（安妮特·贝宁饰）。更棘手的是什么呢？她无法在暴露真实身份的情况下去兑换一张中奖彩票。“每个人都喜欢金玉其外的骗子，”她说道。“她并不是那种阴险狡诈的人。”

斯特普利通过自己的努力（合法的！）获得了成功。作为一名作家，她还曾在家乡多伦多做过体育记者、墓园园丁、酒吧调酒师、旅游代表、马厩帮工和创意写作老师。她的热门小说包括《雨天该做的事》、《为爱而嫁》、《最后的度假胜地》和《假日交换》——还有《闪电瓶》，那就是七年前她放弃的那本难以完成的书（同样成为了里斯读书俱乐部的推荐书目）。该书的电视剧改编正在早期筹备中。

2027年即将推出：《幸运》续作《没有所谓的幸运》，将讲述幸运·安德森的更多故事。“我习惯了写完一个角色就结束这本书，然后继续写下一个，”她说道。“但幸运这个角色已经陪伴我这么多年了，我再高兴不过了。”

在开始全国巡回宣传之前，斯特普利接受了《读者文摘》的采访，谈到了新剧集以及更多内容。继续阅读，了解这部剧与原著的契合程度、为什么你会支持她的女主人公，以及幸运接下来会发生什么。

这次采访已经过编辑精简和润色。

《读者文摘》：你是如何想到《幸运》这个创意的？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我们一家人在纽约滑雪度假时，正在听广播里讲一个故事，说有一张彩票即将无人认领。其中一个电台主持人说：“我想知道，如果你因为犯罪被通缉，却无法出面领取奖金，会发生什么。”我一直在想这个问题。

但我想要一个道德上更加模糊的角色。当时，我儿子非常喜欢看那些《十一罗汉》系列电影。我想到，当我们在支持那些角色时，感觉他们并不像在犯罪。现实是，被诈骗当然很糟糕——但在屏幕上看或在书里读别人诈骗，确实很有趣。

《读者文摘》：与里斯读书俱乐部的合作是怎样的？瑞茜·威瑟斯彭亲自打电话告诉你这个好消息了吗？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：首先，她的团队联系了我的出版社，敲定了细节。我的经纪人和编辑说要带我去吃饭，作为一名作家，我很自然地往最坏的方向想，觉得是坏消息。然后我的编辑说我将成为瑞茜12月的推荐书目，而且我需要保密三个月。我立刻喜极而泣——这本书在疫情中期首先在加拿大出版，但并没有真正引起轰动。

我确实收到了瑞茜的回复。她在宣布前一天的晚上给我发了邮件，告诉我她喜欢这本书的哪些地方。我们一直有联系，也见过几次面。她致力于提升女性故事的承诺仍然让我非常感动。

《读者文摘》：作为剧集的制片人，你真的深度参与了吗？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：是的。我与制片人保持密切沟通。他们问我的第一个问题是关于幸运的选角，而我唯一想要的女演员就是安雅·泰勒-乔伊。在我们与她见面之前，她的经纪人凭直觉觉得这本书很适合她，就把书给了她。这就是为什么她很快就确定出演了。

总的来说，制片人们非常敏感和体贴。会有一些改动，但我相信改编作品必须有所不同，因为有时候直接照搬书本到屏幕会显得有点笨拙。所以我一路都给予了祝福。

《读者文摘》：你能具体说说有哪些改动吗？读者可以期待什么？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我把原著看作是电影的配套读物。实际上它更像是前传，因为我们更多地了解到幸运的童年以及她与父亲的关系。就像我们把角色们丢进了滚烫的水里，但基础是这段非常复杂的关系。

我还想指出，在原著中，彩票是一个大钩子。但编剧们认为，加上她周围发生的其他事情，这个元素几乎太多了。所以现在的主线变成了她在为生存而逃亡。老实说，我觉得他们这样做并没有错。

《读者文摘》：脑海中立刻浮现的最喜欢的幕后时刻是什么？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我去年五月去洛杉矶待了几个星期拍片。早期，我在化妆车里闲坐，看到一个女人完全平躺着做妆造。她坐起来后，原来是安妮特·贝宁。我在脑子里自言自语：哦，天哪，我还没准备好。我该说什么呢？简单点。就介绍一下自己，说很荣幸见到她。

我深吸一口气。然后她从我身边走过，停下来，转过头说：“哦，抱歉，你是玛丽莎·斯特普利吗？我是安妮特。见到你很荣幸。”我告诉她，她抢了我的台词，她说：“哦，真可爱！”然后她就走出化妆车了。

她非常平易近人，这真的帮助我安定下来。我不得不记住，每个人都只是普通人，都在工作，都很高兴能在那里。

《读者文摘》：这个故事的总体收获是什么？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：当我回顾这部小说时，我甚至不太记得自己是怎么写出来的。我和妈妈非常亲近。即使在她弥留之际，她还告诉我：“你必须继续战斗，你必须继续努力。”当时我也在努力活下去，完成这本书需要这种韧性。对我来说，这就是收获：我知道生活可能很艰难，但我也知道当你找到内心的力量、克服一切困难坚持下去时，会发生什么。

《读者文摘》：你能分享一下续作的情况吗？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：它肯定很精彩。我让幸运踏上了另一段追求自由的旅程，我知道她最终的追求是能否摆脱过去的创伤。所以剧情涉及很多家庭困难和救赎——而且这是一场精彩绝伦的环球盗窃冒险。我很高兴能再次与她相伴。如果可以的话，我会写无穷无尽的幸运系列小说。

《读者文摘》：我们现在是在七月交谈。你如何定义一个好的夏季读物？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：对我来说，答案就是优美的文字加上引人入胜的情节，让你能保持专注——即使在假期里周围可能有很多事情。

《读者文摘》：这个夏天你在阅读清单上有哪些书？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：太多了。斯克里布纳出版社给我寄书。我对波西亚·埃兰的《归途》非常期待。我有波塔·梅里迪安的《棕榈子午线》和杰西卡·诺尔的《无助》。当然，我还要读惠理子的《你以前是安妮》。

《读者文摘》：让我们来一些书籍相关的快问快答。你最近读过的最好的书是什么？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：安·帕切特的《惠斯勒》。没有人比她更擅长写家庭剧。

《读者文摘》：你最喜欢在哪里读书？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我在安大略省北湖县的哈里伯顿有一间小屋。那是我在这个世界上最喜爱的地方。

《读者文摘》：上一本让你流泪的书是什么？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我刚重读了伊丽莎白·斯特劳特的《奥利芙·基特里奇》。某些时刻真的太人性化了。

《读者文摘》：哪本书能让人走出阅读低谷？

玛丽莎·斯特普利：我能说《幸运》吗？因为我自己就是在低谷中写这本书的！

加入免费的《读者文摘》读书俱乐部，获取优质读物、每月讨论、作者问答和书友社区。

在《读者文摘》，我们致力于由具有专业知识和经验的作家生产高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等权威一手资料，以及作家在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并在时间推移中回顾更新，确保其准确性和时效性。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。

这篇文章《独家！作者玛丽莎·斯特普利谈全新剧集<幸运>——以及为什么你无法不支持她的女主人公》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
Marissa Stapley didn’t exactly get her spark for Lucky under fortunate circumstances. In 2019, the author was crafting a novel but felt too unmotivated and unfocused to stick with it. “The characters were so complex and sad—I knew it wasn’t working,” she says. Then her mom, Valerie, who was terminally ill at the time, gave her the encouragement she needed. “She always wanted me to push ahead with my career, even when I was caring for her,” she recalls. “So she was like, ‘Well, think of something else. You always have ideas.’”

Stapley soon came up with a gripping thriller about a heroine who happens to be a world-class criminal. It became a bestseller and a Reese’s Book Club pick in December 2021. Now, Lucky has been adapted into a seven-episode Apple TV series, which premiered on July 15.

Anya Taylor-Joy (The Queen’s Gambit) is Lucky Anderson, a scrappy reformed grifter who learned about the art of the con from her ne’er-do-well dad (Timothy Olyphant). After a botched heist, she goes on the lam from the FBI and a mob boss (Annette Bening). Further complicating matters? She can’t cash in a winning lottery ticket without revealing her true identity. “Everybody loves a con artist with a heart of gold,” she says. “She’s not this dark, dastardly person.”

Stapley achieved her own success the hard (and legal!) way. In addition to being an author, she’s worked as a sports reporter, cemetery gardener, bartender, travel rep, stable hand and creative-writing teacher in her native Toronto. Her popular novels include Things to Do When It’s Raining, Mating for Life, The Last Resort and The Holiday Swap—as well as The Lightning Bottles, which was that tough-to-crack book she had cast aside seven years ago (and became another Reese’s Book Club pick). Its TV adaptation is in early development.

And coming in 2027: No Such Thing as Lucky, a sequel that chronicles more of Lucky Anderson’s journey. “I’m so used to writing a character and finishing the book and moving on,” she says. “But Lucky has been with me for all these years, and I couldn’t be happier about it.”

Just ahead of her coast-to-coast press tour, Stapley talked with Reader’s Digest about the new series and so much more. Read on to find out how closely it sticks to the book, why you’ll be rooting for her heroine and what’s next for Lucky.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more books, interviews, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

This interview has been edited for length and clarity.

Reader’s Digest: How did you land on the Lucky concept?

Marissa Stapley: My family and I were on a ski vacation in New York and listening to a story on the radio about how this lottery ticket was about to go unclaimed. One of the DJs said, “I wonder what would happen if you were wanted for a crime and can’t come forward to get your winnings.” I couldn’t get it out of my head.

But I wanted a character who was more morally gray. At the time, my son was really into watching those Ocean’s movies. I thought about how it doesn’t feel like the characters are doing a crime because we’re rooting for them. The reality is, getting conned is terrible—but there’s something really fun about watching people do it on screen or reading about it in books.

Reader’s Digest: And how did it work with Reese’s Book Club? Did Reese Witherspoon call you herself to deliver the good news?

Marissa Stapley: First, her team contacted my publisher and worked out the details. My agent and editor told me they were taking me out for dinner, and of course being a writer, l catastrophized and thought it was bad news. Then my editor said that I was going to be Reese’s pick for December and I needed to keep it quiet for three months. I immediately burst into tears of joy and relief—the book came out first in Canada in the middle of the pandemic, and it didn’t really set the world on fire.

I did hear from Reese. She emailed me the night before the announcement to tell me what she loved about the book. We’ve been in contact and have met a few times. I am still so struck by her commitment to elevate women’s stories.

Reader’s Digest: As a producer on the series, were you really hands-on?

Marissa Stapley: Yes. I was in close consultation with the producers. The first question they asked me was about casting Lucky, and the only actress I wanted was Anya Taylor-Joy. She got the book before we even met with her because her agent instinctively felt like it was right for her. That’s why she was attached right away.

Overall, the producers were so sensitive and caring. There are changes, but I believe an adaptation has to be different because sometimes a book transmitted to the screen can get a bit clunky. So I gave my blessing every single step of the way.

Reader’s Digest: Can you be more specific about the changes? What can readers expect?

Marissa Stapley: I think of the book as the movie’s companion. It’s actually more of a prequel because we learn a lot more about Lucky’s childhood and her relationship with her father. It’s like we threw these characters in boiling-hot water, but the foundation is this really complicated relationship.

I also want to note that in the book, the lottery ticket is the big hook. But the writers thought that it was almost too much with everything else going on around her. So now the hook is that she’s running for her life. I honestly don’t think I would have done it differently.

Reader’s Digest: What’s a favorite behind-the-scenes moment that immediately comes to mind?

Marissa Stapley: So I went to L.A. for a couple weeks last May for the shoot. Early on, I was hanging out in the makeup trailer, and I saw this woman lying totally flat for her application. She sat up, and it was Annette Bening. I was talking to myself in my head: Oh, my God, I’m not ready. Like, what am I going to say? Keep it simple. Just introduce yourself and say it’s an honor to meet her.

I took a deep breath. Then she walked past me, stopped, turned her head and said, “Oh, excuse me. Are you Marissa Stapley? I’m Annette. It’s such an honor to meet you.” I told her that she stole my line, and she was like, “Oh, that’s cute!” Then she walked out of the trailer.

She was so down-to-earth, and that really helped me settle in. I had to remember that everybody is just human and working and happy to be there.

Reader’s Digest: What’s the overall takeaway from this story?

Marissa Stapley: When I look back on this novel, I don’t even really remember writing it. My mom and I were so close. And even as she was dying, she told me, “You have to keep fighting, you have to keep working.” I was just surviving too, and the resilience was necessary for me to complete the book. To me, that is the takeaway: I know life can be hard, but I also know what can happen when you find it within yourself to keep going against all odds.

Reader’s Digest: What can you share about the sequel?

Marissa Stapley: It’s definitely intense. I put Lucky on another quest for freedom, knowing that her ultimate quest is whether she can escape the trauma of her past. So the plot deals with a lot of family difficulties and redemption—and it’s a dazzling globe-trotting heist. I loved being back with her. I would write endless Lucky novels if I could.

Reader’s Digest: We’re talking in July. How do you define a great summer read?   

Marissa Stapley: For me, the answer is beautiful writing combined with a page-turning plot to keep you focused—even when there might be a lot of stuff going on around you on a holiday.

Reader’s Digest: What books are on your list for this summer?

Marissa Stapley: Soooo many. Simon & Schuster sends them to me. I’m so excited about Homebound by Portia Elan. I’ve got Palm Meridian by Grace Flahive and Helpless by Jessica Knoll. And, of course, I’m going to read Before You Were Anne from Emiko Jean.

Reader’s Digest: Let’s do some book-related lightning-round questions. What’s the best book you’ve read recently?

Marissa Stapley: Whistler from Ann Patchett. Nobody does family drama better.

Reader’s Digest: Where is your favorite place to read a book?

Marissa Stapley: I have a cottage in Haliburton, which is in Northern Lake County in Ontario. It’s my favorite place in the world, period.

Reader’s Digest: Last book that made you cry?

Marissa Stapley: I just reread Olive Kitteridge by Elizabeth Strout. Certain moments are just so human.

Reader’s Digest: Which book can get someone out of a reading slump?

Marissa Stapley: Can I say Lucky? Because I wrote it while I was in a slump myself!

Watch the Series!

Lucky

Watch Now

Read the Book!

Lucky

Shop Now

articles.title: Skateboarding Legend Tony Hawk Has Entered His Listen-to-Your-Body Era: “I Don’t Do That Anymore”
articles.content:
如果你是个90后或千禧年代初的孩子，托尼·霍克无处不在。他住在你的电子游戏机里，他出现在《火箭动力》中，2003年他在Kids' Choice Awards上从滑板坡道上滑下，跳进一个11000加仑的绿色粘液池——这句话只有亲历者才能理解。

但在霍克成为少数能够从滑板场跨界到PlayStation、再到Nickelodeon电视台而无人质疑的运动员之前，他只是一个在“衰老”来得很早的运动中的孩子。非常早。

“年长的滑板手到了20多岁，”现年58岁的霍克在接受《读者文摘》采访时说。他记得在《Thrasher》杂志上看到过传奇滑板手马克·莱克的照片，图片说明惊叹莱克“30岁了还在称霸”。

“我记得当时想，‘太疯狂了——他都30了，’”霍克说。

距离那个所谓的高龄里程碑已近30年，X Games传奇人物仍然在滑板上。但他已经不再假装自己的身体坚不可摧。“我深刻认识到自己并非刀枪不入，”他承认。“我们都是人，都会犯错。”

这个教训在2022年变得异常残酷——霍克摔断了股骨。急于重返滑板场，他康复得太快，导致骨头错位，无法正常愈合。这样拖了大约八个月，他才不得不接受第二次手术。“在那之后，我对康复的态度认真得多、谨慎得多，”他解释道。“我不再那么轻率地马上回到滑板上。结果是有效的，但我为此付出了惨痛代价。”

即使到现在，数十年的经验也无法让他免受伤病。

今年3月，这位职业滑板手说，一个相对基础的动作出了差错，他撕裂了髋部的三块肌肉。这个动作本身并不花哨，但他称之为“潜伏的危险”。这可能是最能让普通人产生共鸣的托尼·霍克受伤故事。大多数人不是在垂直坡道上摔倒，但我们很多人都知道身体开始秋后算账是什么感觉。有一天你觉得自己无坚不摧；第二天，你只是倒车时脖子转得太快，突然就有了“还好的一侧”。

“你必须倾听自己的身体，”霍克说。“我和其他人一样有罪——不倾听身体的声音，硬撑着疼痛，直到损害自己的健康。我现在不再这样做了，但我以前这样做到了极致。”如今，倾听身体基本上就是些平凡的保养：力量训练、拉伸、睡眠、饮食，以及每天早上大约“八种补充剂”——其中四种来自Qunol，他与该品牌合作开展了“像冠军一样动起来”活动。“我从未想过自己会需要或想要服用补充剂，”他承认。“但它让我能够在这个年纪继续追逐梦想。”

他也停止将自己与完成首个900度转体的那个人比较。“我现在像个58岁的冠军那样运动。所以这和20岁或30岁的冠军的运动方式不一样，”霍克说。“它看起来只是运动而已。”顺便说一句，能够运动本身就已领先于大多数人了。大约只有四分之一的美国成年人完全符合CDC的体力活动指南，该机构认为这是健康老龄化的关键。“运动不一定是滑板，显然。它可以是遛狗，可以是跳舞，可以是打匹克球，可以是追着孙子跑。”

至于这次活动，要求很简单。“我们正在鼓励人们分享他们如何运动，”这位圣地亚哥本地人解释道。发布一段让你离开沙发的视频，用#像冠军一样动起来 标记Qunol。奖品包括霍克签名的滑板——不需要做踢脚翻板。

霍克本人已经找到了自己的方式。在板上度过了45年多之后，滑板仍然给了他一些其他活动无法完全替代的东西。“禅定，”他说。“一种平静和快乐，这是我在其他任何活动中都没有找到的。现在我比以前任何时候都更珍惜它最本质的样子。因为我还能做这件事——这是一种特权。”
articles.summary:
If you were a kid in the ’90s or early 2000s, Tony Hawk was inescapable. He lived in your video game console, he turned up on Rocket Power, and in 2003 he skateboarded off a ramp into an 11,000-gallon tank of green slime at the Kids’ Choice Awards, which is a sentence that only makes sense if you were there.

But before Hawk became the rare athlete who could cross from skate parks to PlayStation to Nickelodeon without anyone blinking, he was just a kid in a sport where “old” arrived early. Very early.

“The older skaters were in their 20s. That’s when they would quit,” Hawk, now 58, tells Reader’s Digest. He remembers seeing a photo of legendary skateboarder Mark Lake in Thrasher magazine with a caption marveling that Lake was “30 and still killing it.”

“I remember thinking, ‘That’s crazy—he’s 30,'” Hawk says.

Nearly three decades past that supposedly ancient milestone, the X Games icon is still on his board. But he’s no longer pretending his body is indestructible. “I learned the hard way that I’m not invincible,” he admits. “We’re all human, and we’re all fallible.”

That lesson became painfully literal in 2022, when Hawk broke his femur. Eager to get back to skating, he pushed his recovery too quickly and displaced the bone, which kept it from healing properly. He kept going that way for roughly eight months before needing another surgery. “I took my recovery much more seriously and much more methodically after that,” he explains. “I wasn’t so cavalier to get right back on my skateboard. And it worked, but I learned the hard way.”

Even now, decades of experience don’t make him immune to injuries.

This past March, the pro skater says, a relatively basic trick went wrong and he tore three muscles in his hip. The move itself wasn’t flashy, but it came with what he calls “sneaky dangers.” That may be the most relatable Tony Hawk injury story possible. Most people aren’t falling on a vert ramp, but plenty of us know what it’s like to realize our bodies have started keeping receipts. One day you’re invincible. The next, you turn your neck too fast backing out of the driveway and suddenly have a “good side.”

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Tony Hawk (@tonyhawk)

“You have to listen to your body,” Hawk says. “I am as guilty as anyone of not listening to my body and pushing through pain, to the point where I sabotage my own health. I don’t do that anymore, but I used to do it in the worst way possible.” Listening, these days, is mostly unglamorous maintenance: Strength training, stretching, sleep, diet and roughly “eight supplements” every morning—four of them from Qunol, the brand he’s partnered with on its Move Like a Champion campaign. “[Taking supplements is] not something I ever imagined that I’d need or want,” he admits. “But it has allowed me to live this dream into my old age.”

He’s also stopped measuring himself against the guy who landed the first 900. “I’m moving like a 58-year-old champion. So that doesn’t look the same as moving like a 20- or 30-year-old champion,” Hawk says. “It just looks like movement.” Moving at all puts you ahead of the pack, by the way. Only about one in four American adults fully meets the CDC’s physical activity guidelines, which the agency considers a key piece of aging well. “[Movement] doesn’t have to be skating, obviously. It could be walking your dog. It could be dancing. It could be pickleball, chasing your grandchild.”

As for the campaign, the ask is simple. “What we’re doing is encouraging people to share how they move,” the San Diego native explains. Post a clip of whatever gets you off the couch and tag Qunol with #MoveLikeAChampion. Prize packs are up for grabs, including a Hawk-signed skateboard, no kickflip required.

Hawk, for his part, already knows his thing. After more than 45 years on a board, skating still gives him something nothing else has quite managed to replace. “Zen,” he says. “A peacefulness and a joy that I just haven’t found with any other activity. I appreciate it more at its most basic level now than ever. Because I still get to do it—it’s a privilege.”

 Add Reader’s Digest on Google!

Sources:

  • Tony Hawk; interviewed July 13, 2026
  • Variety: “Kids’ Choice Awards’ Slime So Sublime”
  • Skateboarding Hall of Fame: “Mark Lake”
  • CDC: “About Physical Activity”
  • Qunol: “Move Like a Champion Contest”

The post Skateboarding Legend Tony Hawk Has Entered His Listen-to-Your-Body Era: “I Don’t Do That Anymore” appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: This Beloved Childhood Toy Just Released a New Line for Adults—And You’re Going to Be Obsessed
articles.content:
怀旧风潮正当时，我完全支持。最近我花了几天时间和侄女们一起玩乐高、做友谊手链、看90年代的电视节目——我都不知道这些节目已经为新一代翻拍了。我简直乐在其中。所以当我看到我最心爱的童年玩具最近推出了面向成年人的新产品时，你们能想象我的惊讶（和兴奋）吗？

就像几年前成年人填色书的狂热，以及成年人对千年隼号等1,000片装乐高积木和其他童年偶像的持续追捧，又一家玩具公司正在把“推荐年龄”范围扩大到包括你我在内的成年人。

继续往下看，你会发现哪款童年玩具现在可以买给成年人，然后放松一下，花一个美好的下午重新认识这个老朋友。

是橡皮泥。没错，色彩缤纷、可塑性强、超棒的橡皮泥现在在商店里有新货了，专门针对成年人推出。孩之宝刚刚推出了“橡皮泥花艺”系列（Blooms by Play-Doh），这是一套面向成年人的手工套装，让你能够创造出精致多彩的花束，可以摆放在家里、办公室或任何你需要一点“勿忘我”的地方。

孩之宝橡皮泥高级副总裁布莱恩·贝克（这可是他的真实工作）在新闻稿中表示：“橡皮泥品牌始终代表着想象力的无限可能。那种感觉不会随着你长大而消失，它会进化。”他补充说，橡皮泥花艺系列提供了一种“更加精致、动手实践的方式来放慢脚步、沉浸其中，创造出美丽的东西，你可以在完成之后长久地欣赏它。”

这些玩具，呃，应该叫套装，每个都配有多种颜色的橡皮泥、工具和配件、一个花瓶以及分步说明。这很好，因为这些花的细节非常精致。花茎、花瓣、叶子、绿植——全部采用鲜艳的颜色（或者如果你喜欢的话，也可以用柔和的颜色）——看起来惊人地逼真。完成后，你可以用“定型喷雾”锁定设计，这也在套装里附带了。

除了怀旧之旅这个主要原因——让我们想起以前玩过的橡皮泥意大利面工厂——还有一个重要原因就是气味。你还记得吧？公司将其描述为“甜美的、略带麝香的香草般香气，带有淡淡的樱桃味，以及盐味小麦面团的天然而味。”我想我能理解所有这些描述，但对我来说，它闻起来就是童年的味道。（2018年，孩之宝实际上为橡皮泥的气味注册了商标。）

橡皮泥玩起来也很有趣，就像老式的减压球和指尖陀螺一样。放空大脑，把你忙碌的思绪投入到一项需要低风险专注力的有趣非必要任务中，感觉真的很棒。没有邮件、没有老板、没有手机（来吧，你可以做到的）……只有你和橡皮泥。就像一个软糯的成年人填色书，不是吗？这听起来不是很放松吗？

橡皮泥花艺系列目前有六种花束选择，价格从25美元到40美元不等，孩之宝也出售补充包，以防你的橡皮泥用完。这些套装在塔吉特、亚马逊和沃尔玛有售，不过不是每个商品在每家店都有。以下是你的选择。
articles.summary:
Nostalgia is in right now, and I am here for it. I recently spent a few days with my nieces playing with Legos, making friendship bracelets and watching ’90s television shows that I had no idea were revamped for a new generation. I was in heaven. So imagine my surprise (and glee) when I saw that my most beloved childhood toy recently dropped new products—for adults.

Like the adult coloring book frenzy from a few years ago, and the continuing obsession grown-ups have for 1,000-piece Lego sets of the Millennium Falcon and other childhood icons, another toy company has been busy upping that “recommended ages” range to include full-grown people like you and me.

Read on to discover which childhood toy for adults you can buy right now, then kick back and spend a glorious afternoon reconnecting with this old fave.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more nostalgia, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

What nostalgic childhood toy is being released for adults?

It’s Play-Doh. Yes, colorful, moldable, wonderful Play-Doh is in stores now with new products aimed specifically at adults. Hasbro just launched Blooms by Play-Doh, a series of crafting kits for grown-ups that allow you to create intricate and colorful bouquets of flowers that you can display in your home, office or wherever you need a little forget-me-not.

“The Play-Doh brand has always been about the limitless possibilities of imagination. That feeling doesn’t fade as you grow up, it evolves,” Brian Baker, Hasbro’s senior vice president of Play-Doh (a real job), said in a press release. He added that Blooms by Play-Doh offers a “more elevated, hands-on way to slow down, get lost in the process and create something beautiful you can enjoy long after you’ve made it.”

The toys, er, kits, each come with multiple colors of Play-Doh, tools and accessories, a vase and step-by-step instructions. Which is good, because these flowers are detailed. Stems, petals, leaves, greenery—all in vibrant colors (or pastels, if that’s what you prefer)—that look shockingly real. When you’re done, you can lock in the design with a “finishing spray,” which is also included.

Blooms by Play-Doh

Meadow Flower Bouquet Kit

Shop on Amazon

Shop on Target

Shop on Walmart

Why do we love Play-Doh so much?

One major reason, besides the nostalgia trip we take thinking about our old Play-Doh Spaghetti Factory, is the smell. You remember it, don’t you? The company describes it as a “sweet, slightly musky, vanilla-like fragrance, with slight overtones of cherry, and the natural smell of a salted, wheat-based dough.” I guess I can get all that, but to me it just smells like childhood. (Back in 2018, Hasbro actually trademarked the Play-Doh scent.)

Play-Doh is fun to play with, too, like an old-school equivalent of stress balls and fidget spinners. It just feels great to zone out and put your too-busy mind on an engaging, non-essential task that still requires some low-stakes concentration. No emails, no bosses, no phone (come on, you can do it) … just you and the Play-Doh. Like a squishy adult coloring book, if you will. Doesn’t that sound relaxing?

What items can you get?

The Blooms by Play-Doh series currently has six floral bouquet options, from $25 to $40, and Hasbro also sells refill packs if you run out of Play-Doh. The kits are available at Target, Amazon and Walmart, though not every item is at every store. Here are your options.

  • Blush Bouquet Flower Kit: Large, bright pink blooms are the main draw here. Available at Amazon.
  • Garden Flower Bouquet Kit: This pretty bouquet offers a range of flower sizes and colors. Available at Target and Walmart.
  • Meadow Flower Bouquet Kit: Similar to the Garden Flower kit, but with a pastel vibe. Available at Amazon, Walmart and Target.
  • Wildflower Petite Flower Kit: The petite kits are smaller and have more delicate blooms. This one has a wildflower motif. Available at Walmart and Target.
  • Bluebell Petite Flower Kit: Bluebells bring a Texas vibe to your home or office with three bright blue blooms set against lavender and white flowers. Available at Walmart.
  • Rosy Petite Flower Kit: This one is just what it sounds like: a small bouquet of roses in various colors. Available at Target and Walmart.

Sources:

  • Hasbro: “Hasbro Introduces Blooms by Play-Doh, A New Creative Experience Designed for Adults”
  • Hasbro: “Hasbro Trademarks a Favorite Smell from Childhood: The PLAY-DOH Scent”
  • Wall Street Journal: “Hasbro’s ‘Aging Up’ Strategy Continues with a Reattempt at Adult Play-Doh”
  • NBC News: “Play-Doh for grown-ups just launched with floral kits that start at just $25”
  • YouTube: “Blooms by Play-Doh”
